theorems.name: Irrationality of √2
theorems.statement: The square root of 2 is not a rational number
hypotheses: √2 can be written as p/q with p, q ∈ ℤ, q ≠ 0, and gcd(p, q) = 1
theorems.conclusion: The hypothesis leads to a contradiction; therefore √2 ∉ ℚ

steps: Assume for contradiction that √2 = p/q with gcd(p, q) = 1 (fraction in lowest terms)
Square both sides: 2 = p²/q², hence p² = 2q²
Therefore p² is even; since the square of an odd number is odd, p must be even
Write p = 2k for some integer k; substitute: (2k)² = 2q² → 4k² = 2q² → q² = 2k²
Therefore q² is even, hence q is even
Both p and q are even, contradicting gcd(p, q) = 1
The assumption is false; √2 is irrational

logicalForm.conclusion: p and q are both even, contradicting gcd(p,q) = 1

dependencies: parity lemma: n² even implies n even
assumptions: every rational number has a representation in lowest terms
